summary: I was living in Pittsburgh when I first tried to get into the Navy aviation program. The Navy team from Philadelphia came over once a month to examine candidates. Pittsburgh was my home before I moved to Oklahoma City after the war. I remember it as a colder place compared to Oklahoma City, which I later found much warmer and cleaner.

I joined the Navy in August 1942, just after the attack on Pearl Harbor. I went through the Navy Aviation Cadet Program and became a navigator, bombardier, and fighter pilot. I served mostly stateside for training in places like Iowa, Texas, and Florida, and later spent a year in the Pacific on an aircraft carrier. The Navy shaped my early adult life and career, and I eventually rose to the rank of Rear Admiral.

Philadelphia was where the Navy aviation team was based that came to Pittsburgh to examine me. They visited once a month to assess candidates like myself. It was from Philadelphia that the team first denied me because of my leg injury but later approved me. So Philadelphia played a key role in my entry into Navy aviation.

After high school, I joined the Citizens Military Training Camp, or CMTC, which was like ROTC and offered a reserve commission after four summers. I attended the summer of 1940 at Fort Meade, Maryland, expecting swimming and boating but instead learned to slog through mud and shoot rifles. That experience convinced me that when the war started, I wanted to join the Navy.

I spent the summer of 1940 at Fort Meade, Maryland, attending the Citizens Military Training Camp. It was nothing like the brochures promised; instead of swimming and boating, I learned to march through mud and shoot rifles. That tough experience helped me decide to join the Navy once the war began.

On December 7, 1941, I was studying accounting for night school when the news of the attack on Pearl Harbor came over the radio. I remember sitting at my desk, trying to figure out where Pearl Harbor was because no one seemed to know. That event changed everything for me; I closed my books and immediately sought to join the military.

I wanted to attend the United States Naval Academy after high school but couldn't get an appointment initially. Two years later, a neighbor who was a judge helped me get an appointment, and I took six tests but failed physics by just a tenth of a point. Ironically, I didn't need to take all six tests, only English and math, so I missed out by a narrow margin. Instead, I entered the Navy Aviation Cadet Program and was commissioned earlier than I would have been through the Academy.

During my Navy training, I traveled all over the U.S., including Iowa. It was one of the states where I underwent part of my aviation training before deployment. These training stops prepared me for my eventual service in the Pacific.

Texas was another state where I trained as part of my Navy aviation program during World War II. I also flew in the Naval Reserve one weekend a month while stationed in Dallas, Texas, after the war. Texas was part of my journey transitioning from active duty to civilian life.

Florida was one of the many states I trained in during my Navy aviation career. I traveled extensively for training across the U.S., including Florida, Iowa, and Texas, before heading to the Pacific. These locations were key to my development as a navigator and pilot.

I spent a year in the Pacific Ocean aboard an aircraft carrier during the final year of World War II. Although I didn't see combat, I was part of a carrier cruise that took me across the Pacific. Later, I was stationed on Maui in the Pacific as my squadron returned from the South Pacific.

After the war, my squadron was stationed on Maui in the Pacific. I needed to get to Pearl Harbor to catch the USS Bairoko back to the U.S., but the only plane in our squadron was down. So I took the initiative to fly a two-engine plane myself to Pearl Harbor to make my ship. That was a gutsy move that my skipper later remembered when I was a Rear Admiral.

Pearl Harbor is where I was trying to get to in March 1947 to catch the USS Bairoko back to the States. After my squadron returned from the South Pacific and was stationed on Maui, the squadron's only two-engine plane was down. I flew the plane myself to Pearl Harbor to make my ship, which was quite a daring feat. Pearl Harbor was also the site of the infamous attack that propelled me into military service.

The USS Bairoko was the aircraft carrier I needed to catch in Pearl Harbor in March 1947 to return to the United States. My squadron had just come back from the South Pacific and was stationed on Maui, but the plane to get me there was down. I took the bold step of flying a two-engine plane myself to Pearl Harbor and boarded the USS Bairoko to come home.

I visited my sister in Hollywood after the war to get some advice about entering the advertising business. She worked in public relations and connected me with people in advertising agencies there. They advised me to start working for a newspaper in a medium-sized city, which influenced my later move to Oklahoma City.

On my way home to Pennsylvania from San Diego, I stopped in Oklahoma City to visit family. That visit was pivotal because I liked the city and ended up moving there to start my advertising career. San Diego was a transit point on my journey from military service back to civilian life.

Oklahoma City became my new home after the war when I moved here to work for The Daily Oklahoman and The Oklahoma City Times as an advertising salesman. I had family here, including an uncle and cousins, and I liked the city's warmth and cleanliness compared to Pittsburgh. I also attended Oklahoma City University at night and flew in the Naval Reserve stationed in Dallas. Oklahoma City was where I met my wife, Lucille, and eventually built a home on Broadlawn Lake.

While living in Oklahoma City, I attended night school at Oklahoma City University to earn my degree. This was part of my transition from military service to a civilian career in advertising. The university helped me balance education with work and family life.

I worked as an advertising salesman for The Daily Oklahoman and The Oklahoma City Times after the war. I started with small accounts but eventually handled major clients like B.C. Clark Jewelers. Working at the newspaper gave me valuable experience and helped me launch my advertising career.

Alongside The Daily Oklahoman, I worked for The Oklahoma City Times as an advertising salesman. These jobs were my entry into the advertising business, and I managed several accounts while attending night school. The newspapers were central to my post-military career development.

B.C. Clark Jewelers was probably my most famous original advertising account while working at The Daily Oklahoman. I was assigned to maintain advertising programs for small companies, and Clark's was a standout client. Managing that account helped establish my reputation in the advertising field.

Harbour-Longmire was where my cousin worked in the record department, and it was near my office at The Daily Oklahoman. I met Lucille Frances Flanagan there, who worked in the same department. That connection led to our relationship and eventual marriage.

I took Lucille to an Oklahoma City Indians baseball game early in our relationship because she was a baseball fan. Her family had a tradition of going to the games, and it was a great way for us to connect. That outing was the start of our courtship.

Quail Creek was a development in Oklahoma City where my friend Jack Johnson was active. I initially bought a lot near a lake there but later found a better property on Broadlawn Lake. Jack helped me out by releasing me from the Quail Creek deal without charge, which was a generous gesture.

Putnam City High School is near where Jack Johnson developed upscale apartments on abandoned sandpits. The area around the school was part of the real estate developments connected to my home-building experience in Oklahoma City. It was part of the community where I lived and raised my family.

Broadlawn Lake was the lake where I eventually built my home in Oklahoma City. I first saw it at a picnic in 1947 and thought it reminded me of Pennsylvania with its trees and lush grass. After some negotiation and help from Jack Johnson, I bought property there and lived in that home for 25 years with my family.

E. K. Gaylord was the big man at The Daily Oklahoman who eventually called me to his office after I repeatedly asked about dress code on hot days. He was a powerful figure at the newspaper and played a role in my early advertising career. Interactions with him were memorable moments during my time at the paper.
